title: Luka Doncic, Austin Reaves Out Indefinitely; Lakers Won't Give Update This Week
summary: Los Angeles Lakers coach JJ Redick confirmed Tuesday that Luka Doncic and Austin Reaves remain out indefinitely as the team begins preparation for its first-round playoff series against the Houston Rockets, leaving the club without its starting backcourt for at least the early stages of the postseason. "They're out indefinitely. I'm not going to have an update for you this week," Redick said, repeating the message for emphasis. Doncic, who suffered a Grade 2 hamstring strain on April 2, has been in Madrid undergoing treatment at Real Madrid's facilities, where members of his medical team are based. A league source confirmed he is set to fly back to Los Angeles on Friday to rejoin the team ahead of Game 1, though he will not play. Grade 2 hamstring strains typically carry a four-to-six week recovery timeline. Reaves sustained a Grade 2 oblique strain in the same game and has been receiving treatment in Los Angeles. He attended Tuesday's practice but did not participate. His injury carries an identical four-to-six week timetable. With both guards sidelined, Redick said the organization's focus is on extending the series long enough for the two to potentially return later in the playoffs. Redick indicated the burden of playmaking will not fall exclusively on 41-year-old LeBron James. "That's what we've had to do for 82 games for the last two years, so it's not any different," Redick said. "We're mindful. I do think we have other options other than just him having the ball and going against a set defense every single possession." Redick said the Lakers distilled their series preparation against Houston to two priorities. "Take care of the ball and box out," he said. The Lakers enter the series as +550 underdogs , according to oddsmakers.

title: Pelicans Have No Intention Of Trading Zion Williamson In Offseason
summary: The future of Zion Williamson with the New Orleans Pelicans has been an open question for multiple offseasons dating back to the 2023 draft when he was linked with a trade for the draft rights to Scoot Henderson. But Joe Dumars continues to signal that Williamson will remain with the Pelicans. "We have no intentions of doing that," said Dumars on Tuesday when asked about a potential trade of Williamson. "We look forward to working with him next year." Williamson played in 62 games for the Pelicans, averaging 21.0 points, 5.7 rebounds and 3.2 assists in 29.7 minutes. Williamson played in just 30 games in 2024-25, but he played in 70 during the 2023-24 season. Williamson, who is represented by Bill Duffy , cannot become a free agent until 2028. For his part, Williamson has also spoken publicly about his desire to remain with the Pelicans.

title: Stephen Curry, Kristaps Porzingis, Al Horford On Minutes Restriction In Play-In Game
summary: Stephen Curry, Kristaps Porzingis and Al Horford will play under minutes restrictions Wednesday when the Golden State Warriors faces the Los Angeles Clippers in a play-in game, head coach Steve Kerr confirmed. All three will be held under 40 minutes in the game despite the reality that a Warriors loss ends their season. Golden State secured the final play-in spot at 37-45 and is seeking a rematch after falling to the Los Angeles Clippers 115-110 in Sunday's regular-season finale. Curry has battled knee problems throughout the season. Horford has been dealing with a calf issue, while Porzingis and Draymond Green have both been managing sore backs. Jimmy Butler, meanwhile, has been sidelined since tearing his ACL in early January. Kerr has been vocal about the NBA's schedule contributing to the league's injury concerns. "I know this will not be a popular opinion in the league office, but I will continue to say it because it's obvious we need to play fewer games - we need to take 10 games off the schedule," said Kerr in March. "I think it would be a more competitive and healthier league if we played fewer games." For Los Angeles, Kawhi Leonard sat out the final regular-season games to prepare for the play-in. Leonard posted a career-high 27.9 points, 6.4 rebounds and 3.6 assists this season, appearing in 68 games after injury limited him to 37 the prior year.

title: Steve Kerr Wants To Return Next Season Despite Unresolved Contract
summary: Steve Kerr wants to remain the Golden State Warriors' head coach, but his future with the franchise is unresolved as his contract expires at the end of this season. The 60-year-old signed a two-year, $35 million extension in 2024 covering only through the current campaign. Kerr and general manager Mike Dunleavy have maintained a consistent position throughout the season that all future decisions will be addressed once play concludes. A team source recently confirmed that nothing has changed and that all parties plan to communicate openly when the time comes. The Warriors will play at the Los Angeles Clippers this week in the 9/10 play-in game. "It doesn't factor in," Kerr said when asked whether the season's outcome would influence his decision. "That's something I'll address after the season ends. My focus is all on the Clippers and I'm excited about trying to get in." There has been no indication of a split between Kerr and the organization. His desire to stay is rooted in his relationship with Stephen Curry and the culture he has built in Golden State since taking the job in 2014. The season that tested that culture was unlike any Kerr has navigated in 12 years on the Warriors' bench. Jimmy Butler tore his ACL in January. Moses Moody suffered a patellar tendon rupture in March. Curry missed more than two months with a knee injury. Jonathan Kuminga was traded ahead of the February deadline after a prolonged standoff. A public dispute with Draymond Green surfaced just before Christmas, prompting a later apology from Kerr. Despite the turbulence, those who have watched Kerr this season describe a coach still deeply engaged in the process. "Coming in and seeing the guys and having a film session, trying to help them win," said Kerr when asked what motivates him. "And having dinner with the coaches, and the camaraderie that exists when you are in the foxhole together. You've lost five in a row, and it sucks - it's all relative. It sucks, but it's so awesome to be with a group of great guys and try to win a basketball game. I love all of it. And you have to embrace the challenge and the fact that every year's just gonna be different." Kerr has won nine NBA championships as a player and coach and added an Olympic gold medal coaching Team USA in Paris in 2024.

title: Anthony Davis Wants Championship Plan From Wizards Before Committing Long-Term
summary: Anthony Davis made clear Monday that the Washington Wizards must present a credible path to contention this summer, setting up what figures to be one of the NBA offseason's most consequential front office conversations. Davis said he plans to meet with Monumental Basketball president Michael Winger and general manager Will Dawkins in the coming months to evaluate the franchise's competitive timeline. Davis stopped well short of requesting a trade, acknowledging his contractual obligations while leaving the door open depending on what those discussions produce. "Whether we figure something out in the summer (that's) good or bad, whatever ... if it doesn't get figured out, then come October, mid-October, late October, I will have a Washington Wizards uniform on, and I will go out there, and I will do everything I can to win a basketball game," Davis said. "In the summer, right now, I'm going to compete, train and have the mindset that I will be here next year. Not only because I'm under contract, but also, I like these young guys. I ain't going to lie. I feel like they're all my children." Davis was direct about the difficulty of the Wizards' situation, expressing doubt that Washington can leap from the league's worst record in 2025-26 to a championship contender in one offseason, even with himself and Trae Young on the roster. "I've been in this league a long time, and I've been with losing teams, been on losing teams," Davis said. "It's very hard to be a losing team and then a championship contender, right? I think one team has only done it: Boston in '08. Even when I was in New Orleans and we had two losing seasons, then we made the playoffs our third year - that, within itself, is a huge jump. What is our plan for winning going into next season? And how? Not like this is a plan, but how do we execute that? And based on that, we'll kind of see what happens." Davis' contract adds another layer of complexity. He is owed $58.5 million in 2026-27 and holds a $62.8 million player option for 2027-28. Industry speculation suggests the 33-year-old may seek additional years on his current deal, though whether Washington would entertain that given his injury history and the organization's focus on its young core remains unclear. Monday marked Davis' first formal availability with Washington-area media since his surprise trade from the Dallas Mavericks in early February. Despite a rocky initial perception of the franchise, Davis said his concerns faded quickly after arriving. "I can personally say now it's not what people think or what people make it seem," Davis said. "Yes, the losing is part of it, so people kind of tie that with the organization. But the organization within itself is totally different from what people think they're seeing."

title: Azzi Fudd Selected No. 1 In WNBA Draft By Dallas Wings
summary: Former UConn star Azzi Fudd was selected first overall by the Dallas Wings in the 2026 WNBA Draft, earning a $500,000 salary under the league's newly ratified collective bargaining agreement, nearly seven times what teammate Paige Bueckers earned as the top pick in 2025. "I don't think it's fully sunk in. It's nothing I could have imagined," Fudd said. "Such a surreal feeling." Fudd joins Bueckers in Dallas, reuniting the former Huskies teammates and giving UConn a record seven No. 1 overall selections in WNBA Draft history. Bueckers was present at the draft alongside several of Fudd's former Connecticut teammates. "Paige is an incredible player, everyone knows that," Fudd said. "She's someone that makes playing basketball with easy." Fudd averaged career highs across multiple categories in 2025-26, posting 17.3 points, 48 percent shooting, 45 percent from three, and 3.1 assists per game. She made 117 three-pointers this past season, fourth most in UConn history, and 196 over the past two seasons, the most in Division I over that span. Her path to Dallas included significant injury hurdles, including a torn ACL that limited her to just two games in 2023-24. UCLA made history as the draft's deepest program. Lauren Betts went fourth to Washington, Gabriela Jaquez fifth to Chicago, and Kiki Rice sixth to expansion franchise Toronto, breaking UConn's record of four first-round selections set in 2002. The Bruins tied the all-time record of five total players drafted, shared previously by Tennessee, Notre Dame, and South Carolina. Minnesota took TCU guard Olivia Miles with the No. 2 pick. Seattle selected Spain center Awa Fam Thiam third before Portland, in its first-ever pick as an expansion franchise, chose Spanish guard Iyana Martin Carrion. Under the new CBA, second and third-round picks will earn $270,000, a figure that surpasses the previous maximum salary under the old agreement. "I'm just blessed and grateful to come at this time," said No. 8 pick Flau'jae Johnson, who was traded to Seattle after being selected by Golden State. "My goal is to leave it better than I found it."

title: Top-5 Net Rating Teams Each Also Had Top-10 Offensive, Defensive Efficiency
summary: Five NBA teams finished the 2025-26 regular season ranked in the top 10 in both offensive and defensive efficiency, a threshold that historically identifies legitimate championship contenders. Since 1974, roughly 75 percent of all NBA champions posted top-10 marks in both categories during the regular season before going on to win the NBA championship. The Oklahoma City Thunder led the group on the defensive end, ranking first in defensive rating at 107.2, while posting a 119.2 offensive rating, good for seventh in the league. The San Antonio Spurs finished third defensively at 110.9 and fifth offensively at 119.3. The Detroit Pistons ranked second in defensive efficiency at 109.7 paired with a 10th-place offensive rating of 117.7. The Boston Celtics posted the second-best offensive rating in the league at 120.7 and ranked fourth defensively at 112.8. The New York Knicks rounded out the group, finishing third offensively at 120.0 and eighth defensively at 113.4. All five clubs also finished in the top five in overall net rating , underlining the two-way consistency of the NBA's leading teams this season. The Denver Nuggets and Cleveland Cavaliers have better NBA championship odds entering the playoffs than the Knicks and Pistons despite the statistical profile. Those falling outside the top 10 in one category have typically compensated with a top-3 ranking in the other to remain viable title contenders. Teams outside both thresholds have rarely, if ever, won a championship in the modern era. The Houston Rockets in 1995 are the only team that finished the regular season outside the top-10 in both categories as a true historical outlier.

title: Pre-Playoff Betting Favorite Has Won 70 Percent Of NBA Championships Since 1980
summary: The Oklahoma City Thunder open the 2026 NBA playoffs as championship favorites at +110 , carrying a 48 percent implied probability. Dating back to 1980, the pre-playoff betting favorite wins the championship roughly 70 percent of the time, though recent seasons have shown that edge is shrinking in the NBA's new parity era where no defending champion has even reached the Conference Finals since 2019. The San Antonio Spurs (+500) and Boston Celtics (+550) trail the Thunder in the current title market . Between the Celtics in 2024 and the Thunder last season, the betting favorite has claimed the last two titles after a turbulent stretch from 2021 to 2023 when three straight favorites failed to reach the Finals. From 2019 through 2025, pre-playoff favorites won just three of seven titles. That stands in contrast to the dynasty-driven 1980s and 1990s, when heavy favorites routinely delivered. The most extreme favorites in recorded history according to Sports Odds History , the 1996 and 1997 Chicago Bulls at -600 and -500 respectively, both won championships. The 2019 Golden State Warriors, also at -220, represent the starkest recent collapse, falling in the Finals despite carrying the highest implied probability of any modern contender. The Warriors played the first four games without Kevin Durant and fell down 3-1 to the Toronto Raptors. Durant tore his Achilles in Game 5, which was won by the Warriors, before the Raptors won Game 6 to win the series. The Warriors' -220 line in 2019 has given way to a tighter +110 to +250 range in recent years, signaling that oddsmakers view championship races as considerably more open than during the league's dynasty eras. title: Giannis Antetokounmpo Uncertain About Bucks Future, Won't Rule Out Extension
summary: Giannis Antetokounmpo delivered conflicting signals about his future with the Milwaukee Bucks following a season-ending 126-106 loss to the Philadelphia 76ers on Sunday. Antetokounmpo declined to confirm whether he has played his final game with the only franchise he has played for across his 13-year NBA career. "That's a very good question," Antetokounmpo said when asked whether he has played his last game in a Milwaukee uniform. "I don't know. It's not up to me. We'll see." The Bucks finished 32-50, snapping a nine-year postseason streak. Antetokounmpo was direct about where the franchise stands. "We're very bad. We are the furthest away that we've been in the last couple of years," he said. "How many wins we have? 32? This is the second-lowest wins I've ever won. So yeah, we're the furthest away that we've been, I think. So, we'll see. This is what we have in front of us." Antetokounmpo expressed frustration about being held out of game action despite believing himself healthy after suffering a left knee hyperextension and bone bruise last month. He also pushed back on reports suggesting he refused to participate in practice . "At this point? Zero," Antetokounmpo said when asked if he had control over his situation. "I don't have control. Being cleared to play, I don't understand ... from my understanding, coming back to play, which I don't think I ever had any return-to-play protocol, but to my understanding was I had to play 3-on-3 to be able to be available to play. I did that multiple times. I've never in my life denied participation of practice. I don't know who said that, who came up with that, but that's disrespectful towards what I've done for this team and the way I carry myself my whole career, pretty much." Antetokounmpo also pointed to a broader breakdown in communication with the organization. "I feel like sometimes people just don't listen," he said. "They listen to the sources. The main source is me. It is what it is. So again, do not disturb on my phone, go about my day, improve, and come back better." On communication specifically, Antetokounmpo was pointed. "I've always been open, but I don't know if that ... it's got to go both ways. It has to go both ways. That cannot go one way." Bucks owner Wes Edens told ESPN last month that Antetokounmpo will either be extended or traded before he can reach unrestricted free agency next summer. The Bucks also listened to trade offers before February's deadline. When asked about a potential four-year, $275 million extension if he remains with Milwaukee through October, Antetokounmpo left the door open. "We'll see when we get there," he said. "We have [several] months [until then] ... it's a long time. But somebody has to offer you that, for you to sign. I haven't been offered an extension. So, if that is on the table, then I will try to make the best decision for me and my family. But if it's not on the table, then I have to focus on how can I improve my worth and get on the floor and do what I do." Antetokounmpo closed with a measured assessment of his standing with the franchise. "So, if everything goes well, hopefully, the Bucks want me here, why not? But if they don't? OK," said Antetokounmpo. Milwaukee will also conduct a head coaching search after Doc Rivers' departure was confirmed Sunday . The franchise faces a pivotal offseason with its cornerstone player's future unresolved.

title: Patrick Dumont Optimistic About Mavs' Future With 'Generational Talent' Cooper Flagg
summary: Dallas Mavericks governor Patrick Dumont expressed confidence Sunday that 19-year-old Cooper Flagg gives the franchise a foundation to compete for championships, even as the franchise closes the season firmly in the lottery and faces critical decisions on basketball leadership and a new arena. "Having a generational talent like Cooper Flagg should give everyone confidence, our fans, our team, that we're going to get back to the playoffs and that we're going to try to compete for championships as soon as we can," Dumont told the Dallas Morning News. Dumont announced the franchise will begin an aggressive search for a new president of basketball operations Monday, targeting a mid-May hire. The position has been vacant since Nico Harrison's dismissal on November 11 following a 3-8 season start. Acting co-general managers Matt Riccardi and Michael Finley have managed basketball operations since and are considered internal candidates. "Monday morning, we're going to get active, and we're going to talk to a lot of people," Dumont said. "Our goal is to have someone on board by the middle of May." Dumont outlined his priorities for the role, stressing culture and communication alongside basketball acumen. "They're going to set the tone for everything that we do from a basketball perspective," he said. "It's got to be a culture that will lead us to win championships and someone that can build that culture." Sports Illustrated reported Dumont is targeting high-profile candidates including Oklahoma City Thunder vice president Sam Presti and Boston Celtics president Brad Stevens. Dumont declined to confirm names, citing league tampering rules. "I can't talk to any of those people now because it would be tampering. So anything that you may have heard is just rumor and chatter," he said. "We'll figure out who's a great leader. Who has a track record. Who knows how to work with young players and develop them." The presidential search runs parallel to a July 1 deadline to select a site for a new basketball-specific arena and 50-acre entertainment district. Dallas has narrowed options to the former Valley View Mall tract in North Dallas and a downtown location. Dumont cited Golden State's $1.4 billion Chase Center as the benchmark. "I think Chase Center is definitely the leading benchmark, just because of the experience it provides to fans," he said. "The in-game experience, the viewing angles from seats. The amenities that it has." On the February 2025 Luka Doncic trade and the franchise's difficult year, Dumont accepted responsibility. "When expectations aren't met, we really have to take a critical look at everything we're doing," he said. "I really believe this is an extremely important offseason for this franchise. We're going to work tirelessly to get things right." Minority owner Mark Cuban said in a late-March podcast that he regrets selling to the Adelson-Dumont families. Dumont responded diplomatically. "Mark is really passionate about the Mavericks, and I think that's great," Dumont said. "I think he's really, really frustrated about the year that the team has had. And I understand that frustration. "I consider Mark a friend. And look, I always appreciate his thoughts. And I appreciate his frustration. "But one thing I do want to say, in terms of ownership, I think we've been really clear: We're not moving the team to another city. The team is not for sale. We're going to continue to invest in this franchise. We're going to build a new home for the team, and hopefully in the long term we'll be able to celebrate that success for many years."
